title: Wax Crayon on Cotton
stainChemistry: Crayon is paraffin wax + pigment (iron oxides, organic dyes), essentially a dyed candle wax. On cotton, the wax penetrates the fiber and the concentrated pigment stays suspended inside the wax matrix. Treat it as candle wax + dye: remove the wax first (same freeze + iron-through-paper protocol as candle-wax-cotton), then treat any residual dye. Heat WITHOUT an absorbent barrier spreads the melted crayon; heat WITH the barrier is the correct removal technique. Cotton tolerates POG, IPA, 3% H₂O₂, and hot wash.
whyThisWorks: Same two-phase protocol as candle wax: freeze + scrape the hardened wax, then iron-through-paper to absorb residue. Then treat the revealed dye with a solvent tamp + Dawn + optional peroxide on whites. Crayon has more pigment than candles (crayons are designed to draw color), so the dye phase is often the bigger job than the wax phase.
defaultAssumption: Typically kid context — laundry basket discoveries are common. Most cases are post-wash-dryer (crayon went through the dryer and melted onto other clothes). That's the hardest variant: melted crayon on hot clothes spreads before the fabric cools.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Freeze the crayon-marked area 20–30 min. Paraffin wax transitions to brittle. If the garment is already in a load that got hot-dryer'd (common case), freeze the whole garment.
Immediately post-freezer, pry the brittle wax off with a dull blade. Flakes come off cleanly. Work fast before it softens.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Brown paper bag or clean white paper towel, iron on low-medium
spottingProtocol.instruction: Lay brown paper or a white paper towel over the remaining wax residue. Iron on LOW-MEDIUM (no steam). Wax wicks into the paper. Shift paper to clean sections as wax appears; repeat until paper comes away clean. Do NOT iron without the absorbent paper — drives wax deeper.

spottingProtocol.equipment: POG or IPA on clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Tamp POG or IPA on a white cloth onto the oily residue. Never pour.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush for the residual crayon pigment. For stubborn color on whites, a 3% H₂O₂ dab completes the job. Skip peroxide on dark cotton.
Launder hot per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — inspect again if the crayon incident originated in the dryer.

steps: 1. Freeze crayon to brittle. Zip-top bag 20–30 min.
3. Iron through brown paper on low-medium — wax wicks into paper.
4. Solvent tamp (POG or IPA) on oily residue.
5. NSD + optional 3% H₂O₂ for residual dye.
products: Brown paper / clean white paper towel
professionalProtocol.temperature: freezer for wax; low-medium iron for absorb; room temp solvent; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never iron without absorbent paper — spreads melted wax
Never skip the freeze step — warm wax smears
If the crayon incident happened in a hot dryer, check EVERY garment in that load — melted crayon contaminates widely

homeSolutions.agent: Crayon in the Dryer? Check Everything
homeSolutions.instruction: If this happened because a crayon went through a hot dryer (common), the melted wax spreads to every garment in the load. Check ALL of them and treat each separately. Don't re-run any of them through the dryer until the crayon is off — heat sets it harder.

Put the crayon-marked area in a zip-top bag and freeze 20–30 minutes. Or press an ice pack in a plastic bag directly on the crayon. Frozen crayon wax goes brittle and pops off cleanly; soft crayon smears.
Take the garment out and immediately scrape the frozen crayon off with a dull butter knife or credit-card edge. Works fast — the wax re-softens in a few minutes.

homeSolutions.agent: Iron Through Brown Paper — Do NOT Iron Directly
homeSolutions.instruction: Place a piece of brown paper (grocery bag) or clean white paper towel over the crayon residue. Iron on LOW or MEDIUM heat (no steam). The wax melts and wicks INTO the paper. Shift the paper to clean sections; re-iron; repeat until the paper comes away clean. NEVER iron crayon without the paper — you'll drive the wax deeper and spread it.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone or Rubbing Alcohol for the Oily Ring
homeSolutions.instruction: After the wax is off, an oily / colored ring often remains. Dab Goo Gone or 70%+ rubbing alcohol on a clean white cloth and press onto the ring. Flip cloth frequently. The pigment transfers onto the cloth.
Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the remaining color with a soft toothbrush. Rinse warm. Dawn lifts the crayon dye.
For stubborn color on white cotton: 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide for 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder hot per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — residual crayon that goes back through a hot dryer sets permanently.

steps: 1. Dryer crayon? Check every garment in that load.
3. Scrape brittle wax off with a dull blade.
4. Iron through BROWN PAPER on low-medium — absorbs wax into paper. Never iron directly.
5. Goo Gone or rubbing alcohol on a white cloth for the oily ring.
6. Dawn + soft brush for residual color.
7. 3% hydrogen peroxide on color shadow (whites only).
8. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
products: Brown paper grocery bag or white paper towel
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if crayon has been through multiple hot dryer cycles and two home rounds don't clear it.
warnings: NEVER iron crayon without absorbent paper — spreads wax
Never pour solvent — tamp with cloth
Dryer crayon = check every garment in that load
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never iron crayon without absorbent paper
Never pour solvent directly on fabric
Never re-run a crayon-contaminated load through the dryer — sets permanently
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates POG, IPA, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, hot wash, gentle iron heat with paper barrier
Dyed cotton: skip peroxide or inside-seam-test

materialWarnings: FREEZE → SCRAPE → IRON THROUGH PAPER is the protocol
Check all dryer-load garments if this was a dryer incident

escalation.when: Crayon has been through multiple hot dryer cycles and two home rounds don't clear it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Wax crayon on cotton, [fresh / dryer-melted], home-treated with freezer + iron-through-paper + Goo Gone + Dawn + [3% H₂O₂ on whites]."

contextQuestions: Fresh crayon mark or went through a hot dryer?
If dryer: how many garments were in the load? (treat all of them)
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Dryer-crayon is the classic kid disaster — one crayon in a pocket contaminates an entire load. Treat every garment before re-drying anything. Freeze + iron-through-paper is the whole protocol; solvent + soap is cleanup.
